article.title: FDA Approves Eli Lilly's Foundayo, the First Oral GLP-1 Pill for Obesity With No Food or Water Restrictions
article.summary: Orforglipron, marketed as Foundayo, received FDA approval on April 1 under the National Priority Voucher program in just 50 days, delivering 11.2 percent average weight loss while eliminating the fasting requirements that limit competing oral GLP-1 treatments.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved Foundayo (orforglipron) on April 1, making it the second oral GLP-1 receptor agonist cleared for chronic weight management in adults with obesity or overweight with at least one weight-related condition. Manufactured by Eli Lilly, Foundayo is the [first oral GLP-1 pill that can be taken at any time of day without food or water restrictions](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/fda-approves-lillys-foundayo-orforglipron-the-only-glp-1-pill-for-weight-loss-that-can-be-taken-any-time-of-day-without-food-or-water-restrictions-302731485.html), a practical advantage over Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide (Wegovy pill), which requires a 30-minute fasting window.

The approval was issued just [50 days after filing](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity), making it the fastest approval of a new molecular entity since 2002. Foundayo is also the first drug approved under the Commissioner's National Priority Voucher program, a mechanism designed to accelerate reviews for treatments addressing major public health needs.

## Clinical Efficacy

In a Phase 3 trial involving more than 3,000 adults with obesity, participants receiving the highest dose of orforglipron lost [11.2 percent of their body weight on average over approximately 72 weeks](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity), compared with 2.1 percent in the placebo group. The result translates to roughly 25 pounds for the average participant.

While clinically meaningful, the weight loss falls short of injectable alternatives. Lilly's own Zepbound (tirzepatide) produces [approximately 21 percent average weight loss](https://www.statnews.com/2026/04/01/eli-lilly-obesity-pill-approved-orforglipron-foundayo/), and Novo Nordisk's injectable Wegovy delivers around 15 percent. Novo's oral semaglutide pill, approved in December 2025, showed [13.6 percent weight loss in a 64-week Phase 3 trial](https://www.statnews.com/2026/04/01/eli-lilly-obesity-pill-approved-orforglipron-foundayo/), also outperforming Foundayo's headline figure.

Beyond weight reduction, trial participants showed measurable improvements in [waist circumference, blood pressure, triglycerides, and cholesterol](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity), suggesting broader cardiometabolic benefits.

## What Makes Orforglipron Different

Orforglipron's distinguishing feature is its molecular structure. Unlike semaglutide and tirzepatide, which are peptide-based biologics, orforglipron is a [nonpeptide small molecule](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/fda-approves-lillys-foundayo-orforglipron-the-only-glp-1-pill-for-weight-loss-that-can-be-taken-any-time-of-day-without-food-or-water-restrictions-302731485.html) that the body absorbs without the permeation enhancers required by peptide-based oral formulations. This chemistry allows the drug to be taken with food and at any time of day, removing the dosing constraints that have complicated adherence for oral semaglutide users.

The drug is available in six tablet strengths: 0.8 mg, 2.5 mg, 5.5 mg, 9 mg, 14.5 mg, and 17.2 mg, allowing gradual dose titration to the [maintenance dose of up to 36 milligrams daily](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity).

## Safety Profile

The most common side effects were gastrointestinal, consistent with the GLP-1 drug class: nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. These events led [5 to 10 percent of participants to discontinue treatment](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity), compared with nearly 3 percent in the placebo group. Liver toxicity, a theoretical concern with small-molecule drugs, [did not emerge as a signal in clinical studies](https://www.statnews.com/2026/04/01/eli-lilly-obesity-pill-approved-orforglipron-foundayo/).

## Pricing and Availability

Lilly announced that Foundayo will be available through its [LillyDirect platform starting April 6](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/fda-approves-lillys-foundayo-orforglipron-the-only-glp-1-pill-for-weight-loss-that-can-be-taken-any-time-of-day-without-food-or-water-restrictions-302731485.html), followed by broad distribution through U.S. retail pharmacies and telehealth providers. The company set list pricing at [$149 to $349 per month for cash-pay patients](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/fda-grants-speedy-approval-to-eli-lillys-weight-loss-pill-for-obesity) depending on dose, with commercially insured patients eligible to pay [as little as $25 per month through a savings card](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/fda-approves-lillys-foundayo-orforglipron-the-only-glp-1-pill-for-weight-loss-that-can-be-taken-any-time-of-day-without-food-or-water-restrictions-302731485.html). Medicare Part D beneficiaries may access the drug for $50 per month beginning as early as July 1, 2026.

## Competitive Context

The approval intensifies competition in the rapidly expanding obesity drug market. Novo Nordisk gained a [first-mover advantage with its oral Wegovy approval in December 2025](https://www.statnews.com/2026/04/01/eli-lilly-obesity-pill-approved-orforglipron-foundayo/) and has reported rapid early adoption. However, analysts note that Foundayo's convenience advantage -- no fasting, no water restrictions -- may appeal to patients who have struggled with semaglutide's dosing protocol.

The broader shift from injectables to pills marks a potential inflection point for the obesity treatment market, which has been constrained by supply shortages, high costs, and patient reluctance to use needles. Both Lilly and Novo are now competing to demonstrate that oral formulations can bring GLP-1 therapies to a wider patient population.

The approval also drew scrutiny over the use of the National Priority Voucher program, with [reports of industry pressure and questions about political involvement in the expedited review](https://www.statnews.com/2026/04/01/eli-lilly-obesity-pill-approved-orforglipron-foundayo/). The FDA has not publicly addressed these concerns.
